Luke 2:4-7: Bethlehem means 'house of bread' in Hebrew.
1 Samuel 17:48-49: The Valley of Elah is still strewn with smooth stones from its streambed, like the five David chose.
Joshua 6:20: Jericho is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities on earth, with walls dating back thousands of years.
Exodus 19:20: The traditional Mount Sinai, Jebel Musa, rises over 2,200 metres in Egypt's southern Sinai Peninsula.
Genesis 8:4: Mount Ararat in eastern Turkey is the country's highest peak, nearly 5,140 metres and capped with permanent snow.
Acts 9:3-4: Damascus is among the oldest continuously inhabited capitals in the world; the 'street called Straight' still runs through it.
Jonah 3:3-4: Nineveh was once the largest city on earth; its ruins lie across the river from modern Mosul, Iraq.
1 Kings 18:38-39: Mount Carmel is actually a coastal mountain range, and its name means 'garden' or 'orchard'.
John 2:9-11: Cana was the site of Jesus' first miracle; pilgrims still visit Kafr Kanna in Galilee.
Acts 28:1: Malta still honours the event: St Paul's Bay on the island is named after the shipwreck.
Esther 1:2: Susa was a Persian royal capital; its winter-palace ruins lie in modern Iran.
Genesis 11:9: 'Babel' puns on the Hebrew balal, 'to confuse'; the city's great ziggurat may have inspired the tower.
Matthew 27:33-35: Golgotha means 'place of the skull' in Aramaic.
Matthew 4:18-19: The Sea of Galilee is the lowest freshwater lake on earth, about 210 metres below sea level.
Genesis 22:2: Mount Moriah is traditionally identified with the Temple Mount in Jerusalem.
Genesis 15:7: Ur was a great Sumerian city; its restored ziggurat still stands in southern Iraq.
Genesis 12:4: Haran, in modern Turkey, was a caravan crossroads famous for its beehive-shaped mud houses.
Genesis 23:19: The Cave of Machpelah in Hebron is revered by Jews, Christians and Muslims as the tomb of the patriarchs.
Genesis 19:24: Sodom is often placed near the Dead Sea, whose shores are rich in flammable bitumen and sulphur.
Genesis 21:14: Beersheba means 'well of the oath' and marked the land's southern edge, 'from Dan to Beersheba'.
Genesis 28:12: Bethel means 'house of God', the name Jacob gave the place after his dream.
Genesis 32:24: Jacob named the place Peniel, 'face of God', and left with a limp and a new name: Israel.
John 4:6-7: Jacob's Well at Shechem is over 30 metres deep and still gives water today.
Exodus 14:21: The Hebrew name is Yam Suph, 'Sea of Reeds', which keeps its exact location debated.
Deuteronomy 34:1: From Mount Nebo in Jordan you can see across to Jericho and, on a clear day, Jerusalem.
Joshua 10:13: Gibeon was famous for a great pool; archaeologists found a huge rock-cut water shaft there.
1 Samuel 31:4: Mount Gilboa overlooks the Jezreel Valley, a crossroads of many biblical battles.
Revelation 16:16: 'Armageddon' comes from Har Megiddo, 'mount of Megiddo', a fortress guarding a key trade route.
Luke 4:28-29: Nazareth was so small that Nathanael asked, 'Can anything good come out of Nazareth?'
Mark 2:1-4: Capernaum was Jesus' ministry base; its synagogue ruins and 'Peter's house' can still be seen.
Matthew 3:16: The traditional baptism site on the Jordan lies near where Israel first crossed into the land.
Matthew 17:2: Mount Tabor rises almost alone from the Jezreel Valley as a near-perfect dome.
John 11:43-44: Bethany is today called el-Azariyeh in Arabic, named after Lazarus.
Matthew 26:36: Gethsemane means 'oil press'; ancient olive trees still grow in the garden.
Acts 1:9-12: The Mount of Olives faces Jerusalem's Temple Mount across the Kidron Valley.
Luke 24:30-31: The exact site of Emmaus is uncertain; several places west of Jerusalem claim the name.
Acts 9:40: Joppa (modern Jaffa) is one of the oldest ports in the world and is now part of Tel Aviv.
Acts 10:1-2: Caesarea was Herod the Great's grand harbour city, with an aqueduct and theatre still standing.
Matthew 16:18: Caesarea Philippi sat at the foot of Mount Hermon beside a spring that feeds the Jordan.
Acts 11:26: Antioch on the Orontes was one of the empire's largest cities and the cradle of the word 'Christian'.
Acts 22:3: Tarsus, Paul's hometown, was a renowned centre of learning in Roman Cilicia.
Acts 19:28: Ephesus' Temple of Artemis (Diana) was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.
Acts 16:25-26: Philippi was named after Philip II of Macedon, father of Alexander the Great.
Acts 17:22: The Areopagus, or 'Mars' Hill', is a rocky outcrop just below the Acropolis.
Acts 18:1: Corinth controlled a narrow isthmus where ships were once hauled across on a paved trackway.
Acts 28:16: Tradition holds Paul was under house arrest in Rome, free to receive visitors and preach.
Revelation 1:9: Patmos is a small rocky Greek island; the cave of John's vision is still shown to visitors.
1 Kings 16:24: Samaria was built on a hill bought by King Omri and became the capital of northern Israel.
1 Kings 21:1-2: Jezreel guarded a fertile valley that is among the most fought-over ground in history.
Mark 7:26: Tyre was a famed Phoenician seaport, known for purple dye and for founding Carthage.
1 Samuel 1:9-11: Shiloh housed the tabernacle for some 300 years before Jerusalem became the centre of worship.
Judges 16:29-30: Gaza was one of the five great Philistine cities; Samson's last act toppled its temple.
1 Samuel 5:3-4: Ashdod was a Philistine stronghold; its later harbour town appears as Azotus in the New Testament.
Genesis 37:28: Dothan sat on the caravan route to Egypt, the road that carried Joseph into slavery.
1 Kings 12:28-29: Tel Dan marked Israel's far north; its huge mud-brick gateway still stands almost intact.
1 Kings 17:15-16: Zarephath lay on the Phoenician coast between Tyre and Sidon, in modern Lebanon.
2 Kings 4:34-35: Shunem sat at the foot of the Hill of Moreh, near Endor.
2 Chronicles 32:9: Assyrian carvings of the siege of Lachish are now displayed in the British Museum.
Numbers 13:25-27: Kadesh-barnea was Israel's wilderness base, near a desert oasis south of Canaan.
Luke 7:14-15: Nain lies near Mount Tabor; its name may mean 'pleasant'.
Mark 5:13: The steep eastern shore of Galilee at Kursi matches the cliff where the pigs rushed into the sea.
Luke 9:16-17: Bethsaida means 'house of fishing' and was the hometown of Peter, Andrew and Philip.
Acts 9:34: Lydda is modern Lod, today home to Israel's main international airport.
Acts 13:11: Paphos in Cyprus was the seat of the Roman governor and a centre of the worship of Aphrodite.
Acts 14:19: Lystra was a Roman colony where the crowd first hailed Paul and Barnabas as gods.
Acts 17:1-2: Thessalonica is still a major city, Greece's second largest.
Acts 17:11: The Bereans were praised for checking Paul's teaching against the Scriptures daily.
Acts 16:9: Troas lay near the ruins of ancient Troy on the Aegean coast.
Acts 20:17-18: Miletus was a great Ionian port, though its harbour later silted up and left the ruins inland.
Acts 27:8: Fair Havens is a small bay on the south coast of Crete, still called Kaloi Limenes.
Revelation 2:8-9: Smyrna (modern Izmir) was rebuilt on a grand grid and called the 'crown of Ionia'.
Revelation 2:12-13: Pergamum had a vast hillside theatre and a library second only to Alexandria's.
Revelation 3:1: Sardis, capital of ancient Lydia, was the first place to mint gold and silver coins.
Revelation 3:15-16: Laodicea's lukewarm water came from hot springs nearby, the image behind the rebuke.
Acts 16:14: Thyatira was famous for its trade guilds, including purple-dye merchants like Lydia.
1 Samuel 24:3-4: En-gedi is a lush desert oasis with waterfalls and caves beside the Dead Sea.
1 Samuel 6:13: Beth-shemesh, 'house of the sun', sat on the border between Israel and Philistia.
2 Samuel 11:17: Rabbah of the Ammonites is buried beneath modern Amman, the capital of Jordan.
1 Kings 22:34-35: Ramoth-gilead was a fortified city east of the Jordan, long contested with Aram (Syria).
Joshua 11:10-11: Hazor was the largest city in Canaan, covering some 80 hectares.
Joshua 8:28: Ai means 'the ruin' in Hebrew, a name it has carried ever since.
1 Samuel 28:7-8: Endor lay on the far side of the Hill of Moreh from Saul's camp on Gilboa.
Amos 1:1: Tekoa, home of the shepherd Amos, sits on the edge of the Judean wilderness south of Bethlehem.
Jeremiah 1:1: Anathoth was a town of priests just northeast of Jerusalem.
Jeremiah 46:2: The battle of Carchemish in 605 BC made Babylon the dominant power of its age.
Acts 2:2-4: Pentecost falls fifty days after Passover; the Greek name means 'fiftieth'.
1 Kings 8:10-11: Solomon's temple stood nearly 400 years before Babylon destroyed it in 586 BC.
Acts 8:38: The desert road from Jerusalem to Gaza linked to trade routes reaching Egypt and Arabia.
Acts 13:13: Perga was an inland city of Pamphylia reached by sailing up the Cestrus River.
Acts 13:14-16: Pisidian Antioch was one of many cities founded by the successors of Alexander the Great.
Acts 14:1: Iconium is modern Konya, later famous for the whirling dervishes.
Acts 14:20-21: Derbe was the furthest point of Paul's first missionary journey before he turned back.
Acts 28:12: Syracuse in Sicily was a powerful Greek colony and home of the mathematician Archimedes.
Acts 28:13-14: Puteoli (modern Pozzuoli) near Naples was Rome's busy grain port from Egypt.
1 Samuel 4:10-11: Aphek guarded the coastal highway; Herod later built Antipatris nearby.
1 Samuel 7:5-6: Mizpah means 'watchtower' and was a gathering place for Israel in Samuel's day.
1 Samuel 11:4: Gibeah was King Saul's hometown and capital, just north of Jerusalem.
1 Samuel 11:9-11: Jabesh-gilead later repaid Saul by rescuing his body after the battle of Gilboa.
1 Kings 9:17: Gezer guarded a key pass; a tablet found there is among the oldest Hebrew inscriptions.
2 Kings 25:6-7: Riblah in Syria was a military headquarters for both Egyptian and Babylonian kings.
Jeremiah 43:7-8: Tahpanhes was an Egyptian frontier fortress in the eastern Nile Delta.
Matthew 11:21: Chorazin's ruins include a synagogue carved from local black basalt stone.
Acts 27:3: Sidon was a great Phoenician port and, with Tyre, a pioneer of seafaring.
Acts 27:5-6: Myra in Lycia was the home of the 4th-century bishop later remembered as Saint Nicholas.
Acts 21:7: Ptolemais is modern Acre (Akko), whose old harbour and crusader walls still stand.
Acts 21:1: Rhodes once hosted the Colossus, a giant bronze statue among the Seven Wonders.
Matthew 5:1-2: The traditional Mount of Beatitudes overlooks the Sea of Galilee near Capernaum.
Matthew 14:25: The 'fourth watch' was roughly 3-6 a.m., the darkest part of the night.
1 Samuel 5:10: Ekron was the northernmost Philistine city; a royal inscription naming it was found there.
1 Samuel 21:13: Gath was Goliath's hometown; its ruins at Tell es-Safi are among the largest in Philistia.
1 Samuel 22:1-2: The cave of Adullam lies in the Judean foothills where David hid from Saul.
1 Kings 19:19: Elisha was plowing with twelve yoke of oxen in the Jordan Valley when Elijah called him.
1 Kings 9:26: Ezion-geber was Solomon's Red Sea port near modern Eilat and Aqaba.
2 Kings 5:14: Naaman was a Syrian army commander healed after dipping seven times in the Jordan.
Numbers 22:28: Balaam's donkey is one of only two animals that speak in the Bible; the other is the serpent in Eden.
Matthew 21:7-9: Bethphage, near the Mount of Olives, was the starting point of the Palm Sunday procession.
Judges 14:1: Timnah was a Philistine border town in the Sorek Valley, close to Samson's home.
Luke 8:2: A 1st-century synagogue was uncovered at Magdala, Mary Magdalene's fishing town, in 2009.
